coredata.dc:title: Surface engineering of carbon dots synthesized from green sources with antiviral properties
coredata.dc:creator: López-Amador, Alejandro
Jiménez-Muñóz, Brenda I.
Gutierrez-Ortega, Abel
Eduardo Elizondo-Quiroga, Darwin
Luna-Bárcenas, Gabriel
Gerardo Silva Vidaurri, Luis
Estevez, Miriam
Liliana España-Sánchez, Beatriz
coredata.dc:description: Our work describes the surface modification of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized by hydrothermal method from natural sources and their potential viricidal activity. A mix of Aloe/green tea/lemon juice extract was compared with strawberry/ginger, obtaining semispherical CDs of ca. 6 nm, with a high excitation-emission peak associated with the characteristic blue fluorescent CDs solution. The chemical composition of the metabolites present in the extracts generates the formation of O and N species, suggesting the formation of C-OH, C = O, O-C = O, C-N, and N-H bonds. As a result, the viricidal activity of CDs was demonstrated against Herpes simplex and SARS-CoV-2 viruses, obtaining eco-friendly and low-cost antimicrobial CDs with potential biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Defect engineering
Green synthesis
Antiviral
H1SV
SARS-CoV2

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Consejo Nacional de Humanidades, Ciencias y Tecnologías

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge CONAHCYT M\u00E9xico through projects A1-S-17842 (Ciencia b\u00E1sica) and 299124 (LANI AUTO); R. A. Mauricio-S\u00E1nchez (CINVESTAV-QRO), Claramar\u00EDa Rodr\u00EDguez Gonz\u00E1lez (CIDETEQ), Susana Citlaly Gauc\u00EDn Guti\u00E9rrez (CIDETEQ) and Gerardo Antonio Fonseca Hern\u00E1ndez (CFATA-UNAM) for their technical support in materials characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of CDs obtained from natural sources: a) HRTEM micrograph of CDs-AGL, b) UV–VIS absorption and emission spectra of CDs-AGL and CDs-SG, c) Normalized XRD patterns of CDs-AGL and CDs-SG powders, and d) representative photographs of the fluorescent behavior of CDs solution irradiate under white light and UV-light.
Chemical composition of CDs: a) FTIR spectra of CDs-AGL and CDs-SG. High-resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra of CDs-AGL: b) C1s, c) O1s, and d) N1s core levels.

Antiviral properties of CDs-AGL against HSV1 and SARS-CoV2 viruses using Vero cells host: a) viricidal activity percentage and b) antiviral mechanism proposed through the CDs-protein virus inactivation.

head.ce:title: Surface engineering of carbon dots synthesized from green sources with antiviral properties

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de Física Aplicada y Tecnología Avanzada, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Boulevard Juriquilla No. 3001, Querétaro, C.P. 76230, México

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Our work describes the surface modification of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized by hydrothermal method from natural sources and their potential viricidal activity. A mix of Aloe/green tea/lemon juice extract was compared with strawberry/ginger, obtaining semispherical CDs of ca. 6 nm, with a high excitation-emission peak associated with the characteristic blue fluorescent CDs solution. The chemical composition of the metabolites present in the extracts generates the formation of O and N species, suggesting the formation of C-OH, C = O, O-C = O, C-N, and N-H bonds. As a result, the viricidal activity of CDs was demonstrated against Herpes simplex and SARS-CoV-2 viruses, obtaining eco-friendly and low-cost antimicrobial CDs with potential biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The irrational pathogen propagation into the environment and its capacity to generate resistance mechanisms is alarming worldwide. Notably, during the coronavirus pandemic, the search for the development of novel strategies based on nanomaterials has set the standard for manufacturing a new generation of antimicrobial agents at the nanoscale [1]. In particular, carbon dots (CDs) are described as paracrystalline zero-dimensional carbon structures between 1–10 nm surrounded with amorphous carbon domains [2] with unique photoluminescence properties and recently have been highlighted for biological applications due to their eco-friendly synthesis, water solubility, and biocompatibility, capable of being quickly modularly through their surface functionalization. It has been claimed that the biological extracts of plants and fruits contain antioxidant metabolites such as polyphenols, anthocyanins, and terpenoids, able to provide heteroatoms and functional groups at the surface of carbon structure [3,4]. Therefore, the extract's chemical composition could play an essential role in defect engineering during the fabrication of carbon nanostructures for biological purposes.

Although the potential bactericidal effect of green-synthesized CDs has been explored [5], little is known about the potential viricidal effect associated with the chemical configuration of the CDs, commonly induced from the carbon source. The novelty of our work falls in applying surface engineering through the promotion of functional groups induced by the green synthesis of CDs analyzing the impact on the viricidal properties against Herpes simplex (HSV1) and SARS-CoV-2 viruses.

CDs from two natural sources were synthesized: a mix of Aloe Vera (10 g), green tea (1 tea bag), and lemon juice extract (10 mL, namely AGL), and a mix of strawberry (10 g) and ginger (10 g, SG). All natural components were obtained from local markets (Querétaro, MX). CDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 6 h. The obtained solution was filtered (1 µm) and centrifuged at 30,000g to remove the non-soluble organic fraction. The structural characterization of CDs was determined using HRTEM in a Jeol JEM 220 FS + CS. Optical properties were performed in a BK-UV1800PC spectrophotometer BIOBASE to 200–800 nm. The crystalline structure of the graphitic CDs powders was obtained in an XRD diffractometer Bruker D8 Advance. The fluorescence of the CDs solution was observed using a UV lamp at 365 nm wavelength. The chemical composition was analyzed using an FTIR Perkin Elmer from 4000-400 cm−1. Also, the surface modification of CDs by the natural sources was corroborated with XPS Thermo scientific K-AlphaTM. Antiviral tests were performed using Vero cells (ATCC CCL-81-VHG) as host, and strains of herpes simplex HSV1 (ATCC VR-2093) and SARS-CoV2 clinical isolated B.1.1 Genbank: ON457663.1, as models of viral infection. The complete information about the condition of analysis is in the supporting information section (S1).

Using natural products for the surface engineering of CDs represents an innovative approach to shift the chemical, optic, and, thus, the antiviral activity of carbon structures, resulting in the adequate combination of the carbon source (Aloe Vera and strawberry) and the antioxidant capacity promoted by the source of functional groups (citric acid and ginger) [6]. The chemical composition of AGL and SG was revised ( S2 ). Also, the antioxidant capacity of AGL and SG extract was performed through the total polyphenol concentration and the citric acid quantification ( S3 ). Fig. 1 shows the structural characterization of CDs. Fig. 1a shows the HRTEM micrograph of CDs-AGL, with a semispherical morphology and NPs size of ca. 6 nm, surrounded with carbon clusters. CDs-AGL morphology and interplanar distances can be found in the S4 section. The UV–VIS spectra (Fig. 1b) show the absorption peak at 233–275 nm of the intrinsic quantic confinement of CDs, associated with the π-π* sp2 transitions of C = C bonds and the n-π* transitions of C-O/C-N suggesting that the AGL mix allows the CDs synthesis with surface defects associated to the incorporation of N and O atoms [7]. The intensity difference on the sp2 transitions (233 nm) between AGL and SG extracts suggest variation in the graphitic composition of CDs, indicated that the extract composition plays a crucial role in the surface modification. Also, the high excitation-emission peak at 430 nm indicates that the crystal size corresponds to the characteristic blue emission photoluminescence, suggesting the presence of clusters of carbon decorated with atoms such as N and O in the CDs structure [8]. Normalized XRD patterns of the CDs powders (Fig. 1c) confirm the formation of (002) and (100) planes of the sp2 structure of the graphitic structure induced by the carbon source. A photograph of the CDs-AGL solution (Fig. 1d) shows the characteristic blue fluorescence of CDs synthesized in water due to UV-lamp irradiation.

Fig. 2 shows the chemical composition of CDs. FTIR spectra (Fig. 2a) confirm the graphitic arrangement of CDs, with the signals at 2983 cm−1, 1663 cm−1, and 756 cm−1, attributed to the C-H, C = C, and C-H (aromatic) stretching vibrations of carbon dots [5]. The incorporation of N and O species was observed through the vibration located at 3258 cm−1 (O-H), 1396 cm−1 (C-N), and 1110 cm−1 (C-O) under both reaction conditions (AGL and SG). Combining the total polyphenols in the AGL and SG extracts (of ca. 24.23 g/100 mL eq. tannic acid) with the citric acid performs the surface functionalization of the graphitic structure of CDs. High-resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 2b) confirm the presence of the C-N/C-O bonds located at 285.6 eV and the formation of O-C = O bond (288.6 eV), suggesting the incorporation of functional groups at the surface of CDs. The O1s deconvolution corroborated the above (Fig. 2c) and the N1s deconvolution spectra (Fig. 1d). As a result, surface engineering of CDs induced by the hydrothermal reaction of AGL and SG extracts promotes the chemical functionalization with –COOH, –OH, and –NH2 species (see S5 section) [9], a key factor for antimicrobial purposes due to its capacity to generate oxidative stress [10].

To validate the defect engineering performance in CDs and their interaction with the virus through Vero cells as host, Fig. 3 a shows the viricidal activity of CDs-AGL against Herpes simplex (HSV1) and SARS-CoV2. The virus inactivation (after 100 infection multiplicity) is presented after 1 h of contact between the host and CDs (500 µg/mL), demonstrating ca. 99.9 % for HSV1 and 86.7 % for SARS-CoV2, respectively. The above indicates that incorporating N and O species by the synthesis method allows the fabrication of antiviral CDs [11]. Our results suggest that the antiviral mechanism of CDs occurs at the early stage of interaction between CDs and the virus; the structure of the spike proteins is altered through electrostatic attraction induced by the surface chemical composition of CDs (Fig. 3b). As a result, the virus replication into the host is not performed, which is associated with decreased RNA production [12].

Hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from natural sources promotes surface defects in their graphitic structure, associated with the chemical composition of the extracts, such as polyphenols and oxygen species, resulting in the incorporation of functional groups by forming C-OH, C = O, O-C = O, C-N, and N-H bonds. Our results suggest that using a mix of Aloe Vera/green tea/citric acid and strawberry/garlic extracts can induce defects at the surface of CDs, obtaining eco-friendly and low-cost antimicrobial carbon nanostructures with potential viricidal applications.

Alejandro López-Amador: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Brenda I. Jiménez-Muñóz: Methodology. Abel Gutierrez-Ortega: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Darwin Eduardo Elizondo-Quiroga: Validation, Methodology. Gabriel Luna-Bárcenas: Writing – review & editing. Luis Gerardo Silva Vidaurri: Methodology, Validation. Miriam Estevez: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Beatriz Liliana España-Sánchez: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: B.L. Espana Sanchez reports financial support was provided by the Center for Research and Technological Development in Electrochemistry.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge CONAHCYT México through projects A1-S-17842 (Ciencia básica) and 299124 (LANI AUTO); R. A. Mauricio-Sánchez (CINVESTAV-QRO), Claramaría Rodríguez González (CIDETEQ), Susana Citlaly Gaucín Gutiérrez (CIDETEQ) and Gerardo Antonio Fonseca Hernández (CFATA-UNAM) for their technical support in materials characterization.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent Advances on Nanomaterials to COVID-19 Management: A Systematic Review on Antiviral/Virucidal Agents and Mechanisms of SARS-CoV-2 Inhibition/Inactivation
Glob. Challenges.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.P.A. Carvalho, C.A. Conte-Junior, Recent Advances on Nanomaterials to COVID-19 Management: A Systematic Review on Antiviral/Virucidal Agents and Mechanisms of SARS-CoV-2 Inhibition/Inactivation, Glob. Challenges. 5 (2021) 2000115. https://doi.org/10.1002/GCH2.202000115.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from lemon peel waste: applications in sensing and photocatalysis
RSC Adv.

ACS Chem. Neurosci.

Narayan, Citric Acid-Derived Carbon Quantum Dots Attenuate Paraquat-Induced Neuronal Compromise In Vitro and In Vivo, ACS Chem. Neurosci. 13 (2022) 2399–2409. https://doi.org/10.1021/ACSCHEMNEURO.2C00099/ASSET/IMAGES/MEDIUM/CN2C00099_M002.GIF.

Mater. Lett.
